questions.stem: What differentiates NSTEMI from unstable angina?

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: NSTEMI and Unstable angina are very similar, with NSTEMI having positive cardiac biomarkers.
layer1_sourceVerification.additionalSupport: Unstable angina and NSTEMI differ primarily in the presence or absence of detectable troponin leak.

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: This distinction remains current in both 2014 AHA/ACC and 2023 ESC guidelines. High-sensitivity troponin has further refined but not changed this fundamental distinction.

primary.supportingQuote: NSTEMI and Unstable angina are very similar, with NSTEMI having positive cardiac biomarkers

supporting.keyFinding: Troponin elevation distinguishes NSTEMI from unstable angina within the NSTE-ACS spectrum

layer4_crossReference.discrepancies: None. All sources agree troponin is the key differentiator.

questions.verifiedExplanation: NSTEMI and unstable angina both represent non-ST-elevation acute coronary syndromes caused by partial coronary artery occlusion. The critical distinction is biomarker evidence of myocardial necrosis: NSTEMI shows elevated cardiac troponin (indicating myocyte death), while unstable angina has normal troponins (ischemia without infarction). Both may present identically with chest pain and ECG changes (ST depression, T-wave inversion), so serial troponin measurements at 0 and 3-6 hours are essential. High-sensitivity troponin assays have improved early detection but also require clinical correlation to avoid false positives from non-ACS causes (PE, myocarditis, renal failure).
verifiedReferences: Basit H, Malik A, Huecker MR. Non-ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK513228/ [Open Access]

Amsterdam EA, et al. 2014 AHA/ACC Guideline for the Management of Patients With Non-ST-Elevation Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2014;130(25):e344-e426. Available from: https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/cir.0000000000000134 [Open Access]

questions.explanationBefore: The correct answer (Elevated troponin) reflects current evidence-based cardiology guidelines. Key cardiovascular principles include systematic assessment of hemodynamic status...

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: The American Heart Association (AHA) recommends an emergent catheterization and percutaneous intervention (PCI) for STEMI with door to procedure start time of fewer than 90 minutes.
layer1_sourceVerification.crossRef: statpearls-stemi confirms: 'prompt reperfusion through primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) within 120 minutes can lower mortality from 9% to 7%'
layer1_sourceVerification.note: StatPearls-ACS says <90 min; StatPearls-STEMI says within 120 min for first medical contact to device time. The ≤90 min is specifically door-to-balloon at a PCI-capable hospital, which is correct.

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: ≤90 min remains current standard for door-to-balloon at PCI-capable facility. Total ischemic time goal <120 min per 2023 ESC.

primary.supportingQuote: AHA recommends an emergent catheterization and percutaneous intervention (PCI) for STEMI with door to procedure start time of fewer than 90 minutes

supporting.keyFinding: prompt reperfusion through primary PCI within 120 minutes can lower mortality from 9% to 7%

layer4_crossReference.discrepancies: Minor terminology difference: StatPearls-ACS says <90 min (door-to-balloon), StatPearls-STEMI says <120 min (first medical contact to device). Both are correct for their respective time metrics. ≤90 min for door-to-balloon is the standard answer.

questions.verifiedExplanation: The AHA/ACC STEMI guidelines recommend a door-to-balloon time of ≤90 minutes for primary PCI at PCI-capable hospitals. This benchmark reflects the time-dependent nature of myocardial salvage: every 30-minute delay in reperfusion increases 1-year mortality. If PCI cannot be performed within 120 minutes of first medical contact, fibrinolytic therapy should be administered within 30 minutes (door-to-needle time). The total ischemic time goal (symptom onset to reperfusion) should be <120 minutes per 2023 ESC guidelines. These time metrics are core quality measures tracked by the AHA Mission: Lifeline program.
verifiedReferences: Singh A, Museedi AS, Grossman SA. Acute Coronary Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459157/ [Open Access]

Akbar H, Mountfort S. Acute ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI). [Updated 2024 Oct 6]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532281/ [Open Access]
issuesFound: Stem is trivia-style, not clinical vignette
questions.explanationQuality: BEFORE: Generic boilerplate. AFTER: Explains time metrics with clinical rationale and alternatives.

questions.stem: Inferior STEMI with hypotension, clear lungs, and JVD. What should be AVOIDED?

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: In cases of inferior wall ischemia, nitroglycerine can cause severe hypotension and should be used with extreme caution, if at all.
layer1_sourceVerification.crossRef: statpearls-nstemi: 'Nitrates should be used with extreme caution in patients with concerns for right-sided infarction.'

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: Remains current standard. The clinical triad of inferior STEMI + hypotension + JVD + clear lungs = RV infarction is well established.

primary.supportingQuote: In cases of inferior wall ischemia, nitroglycerine can cause severe hypotension and should be used with extreme caution, if at all

supporting.keyFinding: Nitrates should be used with extreme caution in patients with concerns for right-sided infarction

layer4_crossReference.discrepancies: None. Both sources consistently warn about nitroglycerin in inferior/RV infarction.

questions.verifiedExplanation: This triad — inferior STEMI, hypotension, clear lungs, and elevated JVP — indicates right ventricular infarction, which complicates approximately 30-50% of inferior STEMIs (the RCA supplies both the inferior LV wall and RV). The RV becomes preload-dependent because it cannot generate adequate forward flow. Nitroglycerin (and other vasodilators/diuretics) reduce preload, which can precipitate catastrophic hemodynamic collapse in RV infarction. Management requires aggressive IV fluid resuscitation to maintain RV preload. Right-sided ECG leads (V4R showing ST elevation) confirm RV involvement.
verifiedReferences: Singh A, Museedi AS, Grossman SA. Acute Coronary Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459157/ [Open Access]

Basit H, Malik A, Huecker MR. Non-ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK513228/ [Open Access]
questions.explanationQuality: BEFORE: Generic boilerplate. AFTER: Teaches RV infarction mechanism and management.

questions.stem: Most IMPORTANT initial medication in ACS?

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: The initial treatment for all ACS includes aspirin (300 mg)
layer1_sourceVerification.crossRef: statpearls-nstemi: 'Chewable, non-coated, aspirin 324 mg should be given to all patients who present with concern for ACS unless otherwise contraindicated.'
layer1_sourceVerification.note: StatPearls-ACS says 300mg, StatPearls-NSTEMI says 324mg. AHA recommends 162-325mg. All are consistent with '325 mg chewed' as the answer.

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: Aspirin as first treatment in ACS remains universally recommended across all current guidelines.

primary.supportingQuote: Chewable, non-coated, aspirin 324 mg should be given to all patients who present with concern for ACS unless otherwise contraindicated

supporting.keyFinding: The initial treatment for all ACS includes aspirin (300 mg)

layer4_crossReference.discrepancies: Minor dose variation: StatPearls-ACS says 300mg, StatPearls-NSTEMI says 324mg, AHA says 162-325mg. All consistent; 325mg chewed is correct.

questions.verifiedExplanation: Aspirin (162-325 mg non-enteric coated, chewed for rapid buccal absorption) is the single most important initial medication in ACS. It irreversibly inhibits platelet cyclooxygenase-1, blocking thromboxane A2 production and reducing platelet aggregation at the ruptured plaque site. The ISIS-2 trial demonstrated a 23% relative reduction in vascular mortality with aspirin alone. Chewing ensures rapid bioavailability (peak antiplatelet effect within 15 minutes vs 60+ minutes for swallowed tablets). Aspirin should be given to all ACS patients regardless of planned management strategy, unless true aspirin allergy exists.
verifiedReferences: Basit H, Malik A, Huecker MR. Non-ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK513228/ [Open Access]

Singh A, Museedi AS, Grossman SA. Acute Coronary Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459157/ [Open Access]
issuesFound: ISIS-2 trial reference is from Claude's training data, not directly from StatPearls. Should note as 'widely cited finding' per methodology exception.
questions.explanationQuality: BEFORE: Generic boilerplate. AFTER: Explains COX-1 mechanism, rapid absorption rationale, and clinical significance.

questions.stem: New harsh holosystolic murmur with thrill on day 4 post-MI. Diagnosis?

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: thinning and weakening of the septum, which usually takes 3 to 5 days after acute MI...creating a left-to-right shunt and a new pansystolic murmur
layer1_sourceVerification.crossRef: statpearls-post-mi-vsr: 'The event occurs 2-8 days after an infarction'; statpearls-papillary-muscle: 'Papillary muscle rupture presents 3-5 days following transmural inferior or lateral infarct'
layer1_sourceVerification.note: Both VSR and PMR occur at 3-5 days. The THRILL is the key differentiator — high-pressure L→R shunt in VSR produces palpable thrill, PMR typically does not.

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: Answer is correct. However, the EXISTING explanation was WRONG — it discussed aortic stenosis and mitral regurgitation instead of VSD. This is a critical error that required correction.

primary.supportingQuote: thinning and weakening of the septum, which usually takes 3 to 5 days after acute MI

supporting.keyFinding: The event occurs 2-8 days after an infarction and often precipitates cardiogenic shock
Papillary muscle rupture presents 3-5 days following transmural inferior or lateral infarct

layer4_crossReference.discrepancies: All sources agree on 3-5 day timeline for both VSR and PMR. The clinical distinction (thrill at LSB for VSR, apex murmur radiating to axilla for PMR) is consistent across sources.

questions.verifiedExplanation: A new holosystolic murmur with a palpable thrill occurring 3-5 days after MI is the classic presentation of ventricular septal rupture (VSR). Coagulation necrosis thins and weakens the infarcted septum, which then ruptures under ventricular pressure, creating a left-to-right shunt. The thrill at the left sternal border distinguishes VSR from papillary muscle rupture (PMR), which also presents at 3-5 days post-MI with a holosystolic murmur but at the apex radiating to the axilla, typically without a thrill, and with acute pulmonary edema from severe mitral regurgitation. Diagnosis is confirmed by echocardiography showing the septal defect with left-to-right flow, or by right heart catheterization demonstrating an oxygen saturation step-up in the RV. VSR carries 40-80% mortality without surgical intervention.
verifiedReferences: Mubarik A, Iqbal AM. Ventricular Septal Rupture. [Updated 2022 Sep 19]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK534857/ [Open Access]

Mahajan K, Shah N, Patel H. Postinfarction Ventricular Septal Rupture. [Updated 2022 Sep 19]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK534873/ [Open Access]
issuesFound: CRITICAL: Original explanation was WRONG — discussed aortic stenosis/MR instead of VSD. Fixed.
questions.explanationBefore: Valvular heart disease assessment combines auscultation with echocardiography. Aortic stenosis: crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at right upper sternal border...
questions.explanationQuality: BEFORE: COMPLETELY WRONG (discussed aortic stenosis). AFTER: Correct VSD teaching with PMR differential.

questions.stem: Anterior wall STEMI involves which coronary artery?

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: Electrocardiogram Tracing for a Case of Proximal Left Anterior Descending Occlusion (image reference in context of STEMI discussion)
layer1_sourceVerification.note: This is fundamental coronary anatomy universally taught. StatPearls-STEMI references LAD in context of anterior STEMI.

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: Coronary anatomy is a fixed anatomical fact. LAD supplies anterior wall.

primary.supportingQuote: Electrocardiogram Tracing for a Case of Proximal Left Anterior Descending Occlusion

questions.verifiedExplanation: The LAD supplies the anterior wall of the left ventricle, the anterior two-thirds of the interventricular septum, and the apex. LAD occlusion causes anterior STEMI with ST elevation in leads V1-V4 (extending to V5-V6 with proximal LAD lesions). Other coronary territory correlations: the right coronary artery (RCA) supplies the inferior wall (leads II, III, aVF), and the left circumflex (LCx) supplies the lateral wall (leads I, aVL, V5-V6).
verifiedReferences: Akbar H, Mountfort S. Acute ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI). [Updated 2024 Oct 6]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532281/ [Open Access]
issuesFound: Stem is trivia-style recall, not clinical vignette
questions.explanationQuality: BEFORE: One-liner. AFTER: Teaches coronary territory mapping.

questions.stem: Fibrinolytics are CONTRAINDICATED in which situation?

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: StatPearls-ACS discusses fibrinolytic administration criteria. Aortic dissection as absolute contraindication is universally established in all ACS guidelines.
layer1_sourceVerification.note: While the full contraindication list isn't in the excerpt, aortic dissection as absolute contraindication is established in AHA/ACC guidelines and is universally taught.

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: Aortic dissection remains an absolute contraindication to fibrinolysis in all current guidelines.

supporting.keyFinding: Aortic dissection is absolute contraindication to fibrinolytic therapy

questions.verifiedExplanation: Suspected aortic dissection is an absolute contraindication to fibrinolytic therapy because thrombolysis would dissolve the clot sealing the intimal tear, causing catastrophic hemorrhage into the false lumen and likely fatal rupture. Other absolute contraindications include: active internal bleeding (excluding menses), prior intracranial hemorrhage, ischemic stroke within 3 months, and known intracranial neoplasm. Prior PCI (even years ago) is not a contraindication. Age >75 is a relative consideration but not a contraindication — per StatPearls-STEMI, patients 75 and older should receive half the normal dose rather than being excluded entirely.
verifiedReferences: Akbar H, Mountfort S. Acute ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI). [Updated 2024 Oct 6]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532281/ [Open Access]

Singh A, Museedi AS, Grossman SA. Acute Coronary Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459157/ [Open Access]
questions.explanationQuality: BEFORE: Generic boilerplate. AFTER: Lists absolute contraindications and explains why each distractor is wrong.

questions.stem: Post-ACS, dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) should continue for:

layer1_sourceVerification.supportingText: Antiplatelet therapy with ticagrelor or clopidogrel is also recommended (in context of ACS initial management and ongoing therapy)
layer1_sourceVerification.note: 12-month DAPT is the standard AHA/ACC recommendation. StatPearls discusses antiplatelet therapy but doesn't specify exact duration in the excerpted text. This is well-established guideline-level recommendation.

layer2_accuracyCheck.notes: 12 months remains standard recommendation. However, current guidelines now include nuance: shorter DAPT (1-3 months) may be appropriate for high bleeding risk; longer DAPT (>12 months) for high ischemic risk. The explanation should note this evolution.

supporting.keyFinding: Standard 12-month DAPT with individualized duration based on bleeding/ischemic risk

layer4_crossReference.discrepancies: None for the standard recommendation. Nuance: guidelines increasingly support risk-stratified duration.

questions.verifiedExplanation: Current AHA/ACC guidelines recommend 12 months of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT: aspirin + a P2Y12 inhibitor) after ACS, regardless of whether the patient undergoes PCI with stenting or is managed medically. This duration balances the reduced risk of recurrent ischemic events against the increased bleeding risk. P2Y12 inhibitor options include ticagrelor (preferred per 2023 ESC, reversible, given twice daily), prasugrel (irreversible, contraindicated in prior stroke/TIA), or clopidogrel (if others are contraindicated or unaffordable). After 12 months, DAPT may be extended or shortened based on individualized ischemic vs. bleeding risk assessment.
verifiedReferences: Singh A, Museedi AS, Grossman SA. Acute Coronary Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459157/ [Open Access]

2023 ESC Guidelines for the Management of Acute Coronary Syndromes. Available from: https://www.escardio.org/Guidelines/Clinical-Practice-Guidelines/Acute-Coronary-Syndromes-ACS-Guidelines [Open Access]
questions.explanationQuality: BEFORE: Generic boilerplate. AFTER: Explains rationale, P2Y12 options, and guideline evolution.
